Introduction
Sexuality is one of the most complex aspects of human life, with 
myriad physiologic and psychosocial influences. It is a topic that 
may be challenging to discuss comfortably in a physician’s office. 
It may promote feelings of embarrassment and discomfort on the 
part of the patient and the physician, and yet it is one of the most 
important determinants of quality of life. Disability often has a 
dramatic negative impact on sexual functioning, but it does not 
change the inherent human yearning for intimacy, companion -
ship, and physical satisfaction that sexuality affords. The physiat -
rist who has the willingness and knowledge to address issues of 
sexual dysfunction openly with patients will enrich their lives 
immeasurably. The goal of this chapter is to provide an overview 
of sexual function and dysfunction, with an emphasis on the 
sexual challenges faced by people with disabilities. Great care has 
been taken to give equal treatment to the sexual dysfunctions of 
both males and females because female sexuality is often over -
looked both in society and in the medical literature.
Sexual Response and Behavior
Human Sexual Response
The classic model of human sexual response was formulated by 
Masters and Johnson
161 in the 1960s on the basis of a study of 
600 able-bodied males and females. The model depicts both sexes 
as having similar sexual responses throughout four phases: excite-
ment, plateau, orgasm, and resolution (Box 23.1).
162 In the  
Masters and Johnson model, males tend to pass through each 
phase faster than females and achieve only one orgasm per cycle, 
often with a very short plateau phase. Females can achieve mul -
tiple orgasms in the same sexual response cycle. Masters and 
Johnson
161 also noted that females could become “stalled” at the 
plateau phase and then pass straight to resolution without achiev-
ing orgasm.
There have been many critics of the Masters and Johnson sexual 
response model, primarily because it places too much emphasis on 
genital responses and does not acknowledge the role of central 
neurophysiologic control.
17,206,257 In the late 1970s, Kaplan 129  
devised a new model of sexual response with three phases: desire, 
excitement, and orgasm. In the Kaplan model, desire always pre -
cedes arousal and is described as “the specific sensations that 
motivate the individual to initiate or become responsive to sexual 
stimulation.”
129,141,206 The Kaplan model, similar to that of Mas -
ters and Johnson, proposes that human sexual response is linear 
and basically invariant between males and females.
141
More recent research has furthered our understanding of hu -
man sexual response, particularly the ways in which the sexual 
response cycle is different between females and males. The Mas -
ters and Johnson and Kaplan models ignore major components  
of females’ sexual satisfaction, such as the importance of trust, 
• S exual dysfunction after traumatic brain injury includes re -
duced sexual desire and activity, erectile dysfunction in males, 
and dyspareunia and anorgasmia in females.
• C onditions such as multiple sclerosis and Parkinson disease can 
lead to sexual dysfunction, affecting both males and females. 
Peripheral neuropathy, often seen in conditions such as diabetes 
and Guillain-Barré syndrome, can also cause sexual dysfunction.
• C ancer treatments, including chemotherapy, hormonal thera -
pies, radiation, and surgery, often lead to sexual dysfunction.
• Chr onic pain conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and osteo -
arthritis can impact sexual function through joint pain, stiffness, 
fatigue, and medication effects.
• Limb loss can pr esent sexual challenges related to factors such 
as depression, poor self-esteem, phantom sensations, and diffi -
culties with body positioning during sexual activity.
23
Sexual Dysfunction and Disability
AMANDA SEEMANN, MIA ROBB STAHLER, AND KELLY M. SCOTT
• The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth 
Edition, classifies sexual dysfunctions based on criteria requiring 
significant distress and a minimum duration of 6 months, em-
phasizing that sexual issues in patients with disabilities only need 
treatment if they negatively impact the patient’s quality of life.
• S exual dysfunction in disability and chronic disease stems from 
various factors: primary physical changes, secondary physical 
limitations, psychosocial issues, comorbid conditions, and med-
ication side effects.
• S exual dysfunction in spinal cord injury varies based on injury 
level and completeness.
• S exual dysfunction after a stroke is common in both males and 
females, with males often experiencing erectile and ejaculatory 
dysfunction, and females facing decreased sexual drive and 
vaginal lubrication.
SUMMARY PEARLS

from a point of sexual neutrality, and the decision to become 
sexually engaged can result from numerous and varied factors, 
including a wish to connect emotionally with their partner.
17,141 
Sexual arousal and sexual satisfaction often do not occur solely 
through physical means, such as clitoral stimulation and orgasm, 
but may also be dependent on intangible factors, such as the abil-
ity to focus the mind on the present moment and a feeling of se -
curity or psychological well-being.
21 The circular sexual response 
cycle may be repeated many times within the same sexual encoun-
ter.21 Janssen et al. 121 have also proposed a circular model of  
human sexual response that is similar to that of Basson, which 
might be more applicable to both sexes.
127
Sexual Behavior and Aging
The frequency of sexual activity has been well documented to 
decline with age.
71,126 Recent research has shown that the degree 
of decline is much less than was previously thought and that 
sexuality remains an important contributor to quality of life 
throughout the entire lifespan.
71,126 An important reason for the 
discrepancy is that older studies tended to quantify sexual activity 
only as intercourse, but more modern research looks at all aspects 
of sexuality.
71,126 The 2009 National Survey of Sexual Health and 
Behavior found that although sexual activity declined with age, in 
participants age 70 years or older, 43% of males and 22% of fe -
males reported vaginal intercourse in the year before the study.
112 
Solo masturbation was reported in 46% of males and 33% of fe -
males in this age group.112
Many medical factors influence sexual activity in the elderly, 
including sexual dysfunction caused by medical illness, increasing 
frailty, and the side effects of medications. Postmenopausal fe -
males tend to experience vulvovaginal atrophy and vaginal dry -
ness, which cause pain with vaginal penetration.
127 Males have 
decreased testosterone with advancing age, which contributes to 
diminished sexual drive and physical effects that contribute to 
increased frailty.
126 Psychosocial barriers to sexual activity in the 
elderly are numerous, including decreased partner availability, al-
terations in body image and change in self-perception, cognitive 
decline, caregiver burden, and environmental issues, such as  
the loss of privacy experienced in many residential settings.
86,126

these sex differences (Fig. 23.1).141

ual encounter does not necessarily start from a place of spontaneous

Aging patients in the lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and 
queer (LGBTQ) population face unique challenges in regard to 
health and sexuality, including increased prevalence of smoking, 
poorer mental health, and functional limitations.
81,89 These dis-
parities are not experienced uniformly: LGBTQ subgroups, in -
cluding adults older than age 80 years, transgender older adults, 
and those living with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), are 
disproportionately affected. Health care providers must be aware 
of these disparities and be familiar with basic terminology regard-
ing sexual orientation and expression. It is recommended to clarify 
a patient’s preferred pronouns rather than relying on appearance 
or what the medical record may indicate.
122
Types of Sexual Dysfunction
Sexual dysfunction is most frequently classified according to  
the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders,  Fifth 
Edition (DSM-5), which was published in 2013.10 T o qualify as a 
sexual dysfunction, a person’s sexual problem must cause “signifi-
cant distress.”
10 This distinction is important to remember for 
patients with disabilities because they might experience altered 
sexual response but not have a sexual dysfunction in need of fur-
ther workup or treatment.
236 In the same way that spasticity after 
stroke or phantom sensations after amputation do not always 
need to be addressed by the physician, a patient’s sexual difficul -
ties only need to be treated when the patient’s quality of life is 
adversely affected.
The DSM-5 stipulates that sexual dysfunction diagnoses re -
quire a minimum duration of 6 months and that symptoms must 
be present 75% to 100% of the time (with the exception of 
medication-induced sexual dysfunction disorders). The DSM-5 
also allows for two subtypes to be applied to all primary diagnoses 
to further clarify the nature of the dysfunction. The first subtype 
describes the onset of the disorder: lifelong or acquired (meaning 
it developed after a period of normal functioning). The second 
subtype is used to designate the context in which the dysfunction 
occurs: generalized or situational (meaning limited to certain 
types of stimulation, situations, or partners). The patient’s sexual 
dysfunction should not be able to be better explained by a “non -
sexual mental disorder, a consequence of severe relationship dis -
tress (e.g., partner violence), or other significant stressors.”
10 The 
DSM-5 criteria highlight the importance of separate classification 
systems for males and females and abandons the Masters and 
Johnson linear sexual response model for both sexes.
252 The 
DSM-5 sexual dysfunctions are summarized in Table 23.1.10 In 
addition to the DSM-5, there have been a number of interna -
tional consensus conferences that have helped to further define 
and classify sexual dysfunctions.
22,23,26,155,178
Male Sexual Dysfunction
The focus of both clinical care and research for males with sexual 
dysfunction has traditionally centered on performance problems, 
particularly erectile dysfunction/disorder (ED) and premature 
ejaculation. There has been a major paradigm shift in the past 2 
decades away from the psychiatric understanding of most sexual 
disorders in males and toward a medicalization of male sexuality 
(particularly with the advent of proerection medications).
165,209 
Purely psychogenic male sexual disorders are much more under -
represented in the medical literature and are often misdiagnosed 
as ED by health physicians.
171 Types of male sexual dysfunction 
include:
• Male hypoactive sexual desire disorder:  Persistent or recurrent 
absence or deficit of sexual fantasies and desire for sexual activ-
ity, accounting for factors that affect sexual function, such as 
age and life context.
10 Hypoactive sexual desire disorder has 
been reported in 0% to 15% of males, with a metanalysis by 
Simons and Carey
233 indicating a prevalence of 0% to 3% 
from community samples.171,206,233
• Erectile disorder/dysfunction: Persistent or recurrent inability to 
attain or maintain until completion of the sexual activity, an 
adequate erection.
10 The diagnosis is primarily based on the 
patient’s self-report, and ED must occur on a recurrent basis to 
establish the diagnosis (unless there is a history of trauma or 
surgically induced ED).
10,178 Prevalence estimates for ED vary 
greatly (0–5% to 52%). 80,224,233 Results of the Massachusetts 
Male Aging Study in 1994 showed that 35% of males age 40 
to 70 years demonstrated moderate to severe ED, with another 
25% exhibiting mild symptoms.
85 The 2006 National Health 
and Nutrition Examination Study was tailored to create a ra -
cially diverse sample population and found ED in one of every 
five respondents older than age 20 years.
216 ED is known to be 
associated with advanced age, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, 
dyslipidemia, smoking, obesity, and depression; therefore the 
prevalence of this disorder is predicted to grow in keeping with 
the prevalence of these conditions.
173,196,224 ED was previously 
known as impotence, but the term has been phased out be -
cause of its pejorative implications and lack of precision. 185,224

• Premature (early) ejaculation disorder:  Persistent or recurrent 
ejaculation before or within approximately 1 minute of intra -
vaginal penetration or clinically significant reduction in la -
tency time (about ,3 minutes), inability to delay ejaculation 
in all vaginal encounters, and negative personal conse -
quences.10,173 This contrasts the average intravaginal ejacula -
tion latency time (IELT) of 5.4 minutes in multinational 
populations.262 Factors that affect duration of the excitement 
phase must be taken into account, such as age, novelty of the 
sexual partner or situation, and recent frequency of sexual ac -
tivity. Consensus opinions also recognize that psychological 
distress in the patient and/or in the patient’s partner is a critical 
diagnostic component. 6,178,210 Prevalence data for premature 
ejaculation are often limited to self-report, with estimates of 
up to 30% across all age cohorts. 152,207,209 However, when the 
IELT time parameter is applied to diagnostic criteria, preva -
lence drops to 1% to 3%. 6 Approximately 30% of males with 
premature ejaculation have concomitant ED, which typically 
results in early ejaculation without full erection. 111 Premature 
ejaculation is often caused by a combination of organic, psy -
chogenic, and relationship-based factors. 6,210 More recently, 
neurobiologic and genetic variations have been proposed as 
contributing factors in premature ejaculation.6
• Delayed ejaculation: Persistent or recurrent unwanted delay in, 
or absence of, ejaculation after a normal sexual excitement 
phase during sexual activity, which is adequate in focus, inten-
sity, and duration.10,169,178 This condition was formerly termed 
male orgasmic disorder. 9 Delayed ejaculation can originate 
through a variety of anatomic, neurogenic, and endocrine-  
related causes, as well as medication-related side effects and 
psychogenic etiologies. 169 As the least common sexual com -
plaint among males, the prevalence of delayed ejaculation in 
the general population is thought to be in the range of 0% to 
9% and is more likely to affect partnered vs. masturbatory 
ejaculatory responses.169,233
• Dyspareunia: Recurrent or persistent genital pain associated 
with sexual intercourse. Male dyspareunia was eliminated as a 
separate entity in the DSM-5 (compared with previous edi -
tions of the DSM). 9,10,252 This change was apparently made 
because dyspareunia in males is much less prevalent than in 
females; however, estimates do range from 0.2% to 8%. 233,252 
In males who are gay, the prevalence has been estimated at 3% 
(insertive) and 16% (receptive).208
Female Sexual Dysfunction
Female sexual dysfunction is very common, with a reported 
prevalence of 40% to 50% in multiple population-based stud -
ies.12,152 Sexual dysfunction in females has become a focus of re -
newed research interest in the past 10 to 15 years in part based on 
the new understanding of the female sexual response cycle pro -
posed by Basson 17 and the implications of that understanding 
toward diagnosis and treatment. 17,141 In contrast to sexual dys -
function in males, the psychological aspect of females’ sexual 
functioning typically receives far more attention than organic 
etiologies of dysfunction. This discrepancy is partially because of 
institutional bias, but it is also based on a growing body of data 
suggesting that psychological factors correlate more strongly with 
sexual dysfunction in females than do medical problems.26,141 It is 
especially important to remember that personal distress is neces -
sary to make a diagnosis of sexual dysfunction when dealing with 
female patients. Up to 50% of females who report a problem with 
sexual functioning do not have any associated personal distress, 
and thus they cannot be classified as having a sexual dysfunc -
tion.225 Types of female sexual dysfunction include:
• Female sexual interest/arousal disorder:  With updated DSM-5 
criteria, female sexual interest/arousal disorder is a convergence 
of the female desire and arousal disorders formerly termed fe -
male hypoactive sexual desire disorder and female sexual 
arousal disorder in the revised fourth edition of the DSM 
(DSM-IV-TR).9,10 For diagnosis, at least three of the following 
six criteria must be absent or reduced: (1) interest in sexual 
activity, (2) sexual/erotic thoughts or fantasies, (3) initiation of 
sexual activity or receptiveness to a partner’s initiation at -
tempts, (4) sexual excitement/pleasure during sexual encoun -
ters, (5) sexual interest/arousal response to internal/external 
sexual/erotic cues, and (6) genital/nongenital sexual activity 
sensations during almost all or all sexual encounters. 10 Owing 
to the recent classification of female sexual interest/arousal 
disorder as a unique disorder, the prevalence is currently un -
known, and there are limited research/data on interventions 
and treatment.57 Prevalence data for the previous DSM-IV-TR 
disorders of female hypoactive sexual desire disorder and fe -
male sexual arousal disorder have been well established:
• Female hypoactive sexual desire disorder was previously 
defined as absent or diminished feelings of sexual interest or 
desire, absent sexual thoughts or fantasies, and a lack of 
responsive desire.9 Lack of interest was described as being 
beyond what would be considered normal for the female’s 
age and relationship duration and must have been a fre -
quent and persistent problem not related to changes in life 
situation or relationship dynamics.22,225 The lack of respon-
sive desire was the key to diagnosis because many normal 
females do not have spontaneous sexual thinking, fantasiz -
ing, or desire ahead of sexual activity. 20 Despite traditional 
notions, female hypoactive sexual desire disorder has not 
been clearly linked to menopause or advancing age.109,141,225 
Prevalence estimates in population-based studies have been 
typically quoted at 24% to 43% but may be much lower 
when strict diagnostic criteria are applied (closer to 5.4–
13.6%).225 Hypoactive sexual desire disorder has been as -
sociated with lower health-related quality of life, lower 
general happiness and satisfaction with partners, and more 
frequent negative emotional states.191
• Female sexual arousal disorder was previously defined as 
persistent or recurrent inability to attain or maintain until 
completion of the sexual activity and sufficient sexual ex -
citement, which may be expressed as a lack of subjective 
excitement or as a lack of adequate genital (lubrication-
swelling) or other somatic response. 9,23 The prevalence has 
been estimated to be 6% to 21%. 233
• Female orgasmic disorder: Persistent or recurrent lack of orgasm, 
markedly diminished intensity of orgasmic sensations, or 
marked delay of orgasm from any type of stimulation despite 
the self-report of high sexual arousal/excitement.10,26 Orgasmic 
disorder in females is often difficult to distinguish from an 
inadequate arousal response without a careful history because 
females with decreased arousal will also not be able to achieve 
orgasm.141 Many females are situationally orgasmic, in that 
they can reliably achieve orgasm with some forms of stimula -
tion but not others. Vaginal intercourse alone is not a reliable 
way for many females to achieve orgasm, and the need  
for clitoral stimulation to achieve orgasm is not considered 
abnormal.141 The prevalence of female orgasmic disorder was

• Genitopelvic pain/penetration disorder: Defined by persistent or 
recurrent difficulties with vaginal penetration, genitopelvic 
pain during vaginal intercourse or other penetration attempts, 
fear or anxiety of genitopelvic pain or vaginal penetration, 
and/or increased pelvic floor tension during attempted vaginal 
penetration.10 This disorder encompasses the former DSM-IV-
TR disorders of dyspareunia (persistent or recurrent pain with 
attempted or complete vaginal entry and/or penile vaginal in -
tercourse) and vaginismus (the persistent or recurrent difficul-
ties of the female to allow vaginal entry of a penis, a finger, 
and/or any object, despite the expressed wish to do so). 9,22 
Vaginismus was originally defined as persistent spasm of the 
outer third of the vaginal musculature, which interferes with 
vaginal penetration. More recent research and consensus opin-
ion dispute the classic definition because muscle spasm and 
even pain are not always present. 22,141,201 Prevalence rates for 
vaginismus have been reported at 1% to 6%. 155 Vaginismus is 
one of many potential causes of dyspareunia. Dyspareunia was 
previously thought to be primarily of psychogenic origin, but 
it is now known that biologic factors often contribute to the 
presentation.141 Some even characterize dyspareunia as a pain 
disorder that interferes with sexuality instead of a sexual disor-
der characterized by pain. 34,141 The differential diagnosis of 
dyspareunia in females includes vulvovaginal atrophy, inade -
quate lubrication, vaginitis, postmenopausal vaginal dryness, 
pelvic floor myofascial pain or dysfunction, vulvodynia (vulvar 
pain without visible signs of pathology and with no known 
etiologic diagnosis), and vestibulodynia/vulvar vestibulitis syn-
drome (a type of vulvodynia localized only to the vulvar vesti -
bule, which may or may not have associated inflammatory 
changes visible on physical examination). 25,100,114,141 Urethral 
disorders, cystitis, bladder pain syndrome (formerly known as 
chronic interstitial cystitis), anatomic variations, pelvic adhe -
sions, infections, endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, 
abdominal wall pain, cancer, late effects of pelvic radiation, 
vulvovaginal dermatologic diseases (such as lichen sclerosis or 
lichen planus), and pelvic congestion syndrome can also cause 
chronic dyspareunia.25,141,264 Dyspareunia prevalence estimates 
range from 3% to 18% in the general population, 3% to 46% 
in a primary care setting, and 9% to 21% in postmenopausal 
females.233
Sexual Dysfunction in Disability and  
Chronic Disease
The development of sexual dysfunction after the onset of disabil -
ity is usually related to myriad diverse etiologic factors that can be 
grouped into five different categories: primary physical changes, 
secondary physical limitations, psychosocial contributions, the 
effect of comorbid conditions, and medication-related factors. 
Primary physical changes derive from the disease pathophysiology 
and directly affect sexual response. These primary physical changes 
could include altered genital sensation, decreased vaginal lubrica -
tion, ED, or anorgasmia. Secondary physical limitations also play 
a significant role in the development of sexual difficulties after 
disability. These are indirect effects of the disease process that do 
not affect genital function or sexual response but which make 
sexual activity unpleasant or challenging. Examples of secondary 
physical limitations include fatigue, pain, weakness, spasticity, 
contractures, ataxia, bowel and bladder dysfunction, and cogni -
tive impairment. The psychosocial contribution to the develop -
ment of sexual dysfunction includes psychological, emotional, 
relational, situational, social, and cultural factors. People with 
disabilities often have difficulty adjusting to their new life circum-
stances. They can have poor self-esteem and body image, fear of 
isolation and abandonment, anger, shame, guilt, strained relation-
ships with loved ones who may also now be their caregivers, 
anxiety, and depression. All these issues can affect sexual function-
ing. In addition, the pathology of comorbid conditions such as 
diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and depression can affect sexual 
dysfunction independent of the primary pathology. Lastly, many 
medications commonly prescribed for people with disabilities can 
lead to the development of sexual dysfunction. It is important for 
the physiatrist to consider potential etiologies from all five catego-
ries when diagnosing and treating a disabled patient presenting 
with sexual dysfunction. (Disability-specific sexual dysfunctions 
are discussed later.) The physiatrist should also consider the im -
pact of sexual dysfunction on the patient’s partner. Partners may 
feel overwhelmed taking on a new role as caregiver for someone 
they are romantically involved with. They may experience chal -
lenges alternating between a caregiver and a sexual partner and 
may experience emotional distress with this. The physician should 
be mindful of this potential conflict when evaluating the patient 
for sexual dysfunction.
Spinal Cord Injury
In contrast to many other disabilities, sexual dysfunction in pa -
tients with spinal cord injury (SCI) has been well studied in both 
sexes. The type of sexual dysfunction the patient will ultimately 
experience depends in large part on the spinal cord level and the 
degree of completeness of the injury. 177 Multiple studies have 
shown that the frequency of sexual activity and the level of sexual 
satisfaction decrease in both males and females after sustaining 
SCI.4,54,63,146,238 Sexual dysfunction after SCI may be caused by 
changes in desire or perceived desirability, concern regarding neu-
rogenic bowel or bladder, genital arousal disorders, ejaculatory 
dysfunction, orgasmic disorders, and issues of pain.144,247 In stud-
ies of males with SCI, the main determinants of a positive sex life 
after injury have included the willingness to adapt their sexual life 
to injury and the experience of physical pleasure during foreplay 
and intercourse.243
Upper motor neuron (UMN) SCI allows for reflexogenic erec-
tion to occur in males because the parasympathetic sacral reflex 
arc is intact. Psychogenic erection, however, is usually not possible 
unless the injury is incomplete. Ejaculation is difficult for these 
patients because it is sympathetically mediated from T11 to L2. 
Lower motor neuron (LMN) SCI has preserved integrity of the 
thoracolumbar sympathetic outflow tract, and thus psychogenic 
erections and ejaculation are theoretically more likely to be intact, 
but reflexogenic erections are not.
Bors and Comarr40 conducted a pivotal study in 1960 detail -
ing the prevalence of ED and orgasmic dysfunction in 529 males 
with SCI. They found that in males with complete UMN lesions, 
93% of patients were able to achieve reflexogenic erections, and 
none was able to attain psychogenic erections. Anterograde ejacu-
lation in complete UMN SCI was seen in 4% of patients. Males 
with incomplete UMN SCI achieved erection 99% of the time, 
with 80% of males having only reflexogenic erections and 19% 
having combined reflex and psychogenic erections. Ejaculation

study are summarized in Figs. 23.2 and 23.3.
Orgasmic ability has been shown to be preserved in 38% to 
50% of males with complete UMN SCI, 78% to 84% of males 
with incomplete UMN injury, and 0% of males with complete 
LMN injury.
4,5,194,235 It should be noted, however, that a majority 
of studies on males have focused on the ability to ejaculate instead 
of the ability to have an orgasm, which have more recently been 
noted to be separate occurrences.
5 Some males are a lso occasion-
ally able to achieve orgasm without anterograde ejaculation, pos -
sibly indicating either anejaculation or retrograde ejaculation into 
the bladder.
235
Females with SCI have been shown to have similar sexual  
responses to males with arousal and vaginal lubrication as the  
female correlates to penile erection.63,239,240 In females with com-
plete UMN injuries, reflexogenic, but not psychogenic, lubrica-
tion is preserved.
63,240 Sensation to light touch and pinprick in the 
T11 to L2 dermatomes were found to be predictive of the ability 
for psychogenic vaginal lubrication, which is thought to be sym -
pathetically mediated.
63,239 From 44% to 54% of females with 
SCI have been shown to be able to achieve orgasm, although or-
gasm is much less likely in females with LMN injuries affecting 
the S3 to S5 segments.
54,63,120,238,239 Self-exploration should be 
encouraged because studies suggest females with SCI may be able 
to achieve orgasm through stimulation of the anterior vaginal 
wall, cervix, and/or nipples.
61,200,267
Fertility in males with SCI is impaired in part because of a 
decreased ability to ejaculate. 151,200 Semen quality has also been 
found to be poor in males with SCI, with decreased sperm motil-
ity, decreased mitochondrial activity, and increased sperm DNA 
fragmentation.
203 Reasons for altered semen quality have been 
postulated to include seminal fluid stasis, testicular hyperthermia, 
recurrent genitourinary tract infections, and hormonal dysfunc -
tion.
151,203 After initial postinjury decline, semen quality appears 
to plateau in the chronic postinjury period. 41,118 Fertility in fe -
males with SCI is preserved once menstruation resumes, on aver -
age about 5 months postinjury. 13,251 Family planning counseling 
should include these post-SCI fertility considerations.
Stroke
Stroke is the world’s fifth leading cause of death and the leading 
cause of adult disability.
251 Sexual dysfunction after stroke is a 
common finding in both sexes. The most common findings for 
males are ED and ejaculatory dysfunction (in 40–50%) and de -
creased sexual drive.
52,124,143,159,254 Women tend to experience 
decreased sexual drive, decreased vaginal lubrication (in approxi-
mately 50%), decreased orgasm (in approximately 20–30%), and 
decreased overall sexual satisfaction.
52,138,143,159,254 Prevalence of 
decreased sexual drive has been reported to be between approxi-
mately 25% and 50% in both sexes after stroke, with similar ef -
fects in spouses.
138,143 Stroke also leads to significantly decreased 
frequency of sexual activity in both males and females, and in one 
study, approximately 50% of stroke survivors reported no sexual 
activity whatsoever by 1 year poststroke.
96,143,254
The physical effects of stroke that contribute to sexual dysfunc-
tion include hemiparesis with its effect on body positioning and 
movement, hemineglect, hemianopsia, neurogenic bowel and 
bladder, spasticity, cognitive dysfunctions, communication con -
straints, and hygiene.
179,182,200 Not much is known about the 
functional neuroanatomy of sexual behavior and control, but 
some studies have indicated that the right cerebral hemisphere 
may play a more important role in sexual functioning than the  
left side.

cally with ED. 51,125 Psychological factors also contribute to the 
etiology of poststroke sexual dysfunction, including depression, 
anxiety, emotional lability, and changes in self-image, including 
perceived decline in sexual appeal. 55,125,136,153,197 Many studies 
have suggested that sexual dysfunction after stroke is related to  
the presence of medical comorbidities (cardiac disease, diabetes, 
hypercholesterolemia, and depression), medications, and psycho -
social factors (inability to discuss sexuality with the partner, un -
willingness for sexual activity, fear of another stroke, and sexuality 
being unimportant) rather than the direct effect of stroke. 182
Traumatic Brain Injury
The prevalence of sexual dysfunction after traumatic brain injury 
(TBI) has been reported at 4% to 71%, this wide range likely at -
tributed to the limited number of quality studies and the varied 
types and severities of injury.218 In one TBI model systems multi-
center study of post-TBI community-dwelling individuals, 20% 
met criteria for sexual dysfunction. 219 Greater impairments were 
correlated with female sex, older age, higher TBI severity, and 
decreased social participation. 219 Although sexual dysfunction 
prevalence and type may be associated with both global and focal 
brain tissue injury, previous studies have not shown a consistent 
relationship to injury severity and sexual dysfunction. 39,200,218 
Some researchers have postulated that hypopituitarism after TBI 
may have a larger contribution to sexual dysfunction than previ -
ously thought.105,123,167 Types of sexual dysfunction reported have 
been similar to those seen in patients with stroke, including re -
duced sexual desire and frequency of sexual activity in both sexes, 
ED and ejaculatory dysfunction in males, and dyspareunia, anor-
gasmia, and reduced lubrication in females. 113,145,200,218,219 In  
addition, brain injury can cause hypersexual behavior, such as 
excessive masturbation. This can occur in particular with injury to 
the limbic system or prefrontal regions, which can cause disinhi -
bition; or to bilateral temporal poles, which produces the Klüver-
Bucy syndrome of hypersexuality and hyperorality. 44,154,234 This 
hypersexual behavior can be treated with anticonvulsants, such as 
Depakote, but these medications can also greatly contribute to 
the sexual dysfunction witnessed after brain injury.99 Psychosocial 
factors appear to play a major role in sexual functioning after 
brain injury with the presence of depression being the most sensi-
tive indicator for sexual dysfunction.113 Other important psycho-
logical factors include perceived health status and quality of life, 
low self-esteem, anxiety, and perceived decline in personal sex 
appeal.113,147 Studies focusing on the partners and spouses of 
moderate to severe TBI survivors have also found disturbed sexual 
functioning for these individuals caused by a variety of psychoso-
cial factors, including assuming a caregiver role, communication 
issues, and patient neuropsychiatric symptoms leading to a sense 
of “living with a stranger.”35,97,103
Multiple Sclerosis
Multiple sclerosis (MS)–induced sexual dysfunction is present in 
40% to 80% of females and 50% to 90% of males.72 Historically, 
sexual dysfunction has been attributed directly to the location and 
duration of the spinal cord or brain lesion, but more recent re -
search tends to describe a multitude of other factors that also 
contribute to the development of sexual dysfunction in MS.45,150,245 
Psychological and psychosocial causes have been proposed as pri -
mary drivers of sexual dysfunction in this population, although in 
males an association has been found between insular lesions and 
ED.244,268 Research looking at health-related quality of life param-
eters in patients with MS demonstrated that sexual dysfunction 
had a more significant impact than severity of physical disability.221
In MS, sexual dysfunction is now conceptualized into three 
levels of dysfunction.45 Primary sexual dysfunction relates to neu-
rologic impairments in libido, lubrication, and orgasm. Second -
ary sexual dysfunction includes the effects of fatigue and physical 
limitations on sexual function. Physical limitations may include 
bowel and bladder dysfunction, weakness, incoordination, posi -
tioning and body control during sexual encounters, numbness, 
paresthesias, pain, and cognitive impairment.150,245 T ertiary sexual 
dysfunction reflects the psychological, emotional, social, and cul -
tural implications of MS on sexual function. Psychosocial factors 
contributing to tertiary sexual dysfunction include poor self-  
image, poor self-esteem, fear of isolation and abandonment, 
shame, dependency on the partner to provide for one’s basic 
needs, and depression. 222,245,261 In addition, medication side ef -
fects may contribute to sexual dysfunction.
Females with MS tend to have decreased sexual desire, anor -
gasmia, decreased vaginal lubrication, and increased spasticity 
with sexual activity.65,116,200 Many of these symptoms are thought 
to be related to decreased genital sensitivity experienced by 62% 
of females with advanced MS.116 Males with MS tend to have ED, 
ejaculatory dysfunction (premature, delayed, or absent), orgasmic 
dysfunction, decreased genital sensation, and decreased sexual 
drive.65,200 In one study, sexual dysfunction in males with MS was 
found to be correlated with lower-limb disability and bladder 
dysfunction, whereas in females, sexual disorders were most 
strongly correlated with fatigue.88 Females report more symptoms 
of sexual dysfunction, whereas males report more dissatisfaction 
with sexual function.45,186
Other Neurologic Disorders
Sexual dysfunction is a common but frequently overlooked non -
motor symptom in Parkinson disease (PD) that significantly af -
fects a patient’s quality of life. 139,220 Risk factors associated with 
developing sexual dysfunction include advanced age, male sex, 
and hormone deficiencies. 184 PD is often associated with low 
testosterone levels and therefore decreased sexual drive. 179 In one 
study of young males with PD (ages 36–56 years), up to 40% had 
low sexual desire.265 Males with PD also experience ED and prema-
ture or delayed ejaculation.46,200 In females, decreased lubrication, 
involuntary urination, decreased libido, and orgasmic dysfunc -
tion can occur during intercourse.46,184 In contrast, dopamine and 
dopamine agonist medications have been documented to cause 
hypersexual behavior, which often accompanies mania.259 Hyper-
sexuality has also been described with deep brain stimulation of 
the subthalamic nucleus.204
Peripheral neuropathy can cause sexual dysfunction with most 
common etiologies, including diabetes, amyloid, and some inher-
ited neuropathies with urogenital symptoms as prominent early 
clinical manifestations. 200 Guillain-Barré syndrome has been 
linked to ED when there is residual neurologic deficit or disability 
after recovery.50
Cancer
Owing to medical breakthroughs and technologic advancements 
in the diagnosis and treatment of cancer, there has been a signifi -
cant increase in cancer survivorship in the United States. As of

2000, close to 10 million cancer survivors were alive in the United 
States, with approximately 62% of cancer survivors expected to 
live at least 5 years from their date of diagnosis.92 The majority of 
research on sexual dysfunction has been in regard to prostate and 
bladder cancer in males and breast and gynecologic cancers in 
females. However, patients with any cancer are susceptible to 
sexual side effects related to their cancer diagnosis or treatments.76
Chemotherapy, hormonal treatments, radiation therapy, and 
surgical procedures can all affect sexual function in both sexes. 
Common chemotherapy agent side effects (fatigue, weakness, nau-
sea, diarrhea) can contribute to decreased sexual desire. Addition-
ally, chemotherapy and endocrine therapies have the capacity to 
induce ovarian failure and premature menopausal symptoms in 
females.76 Removal of pelvic organs in females can cause irrevers -
ible menopause (in the case of oophorectomy), anatomic changes, 
dyspareunia, and a sense of loss regarding one’s sexuality.250 Both 
lumpectomy and mastectomy for breast cancer have been associ -
ated with decreased positive sexuality owing to both physiologic 
and psychological sequelae. 76 In females, radiotherapy for breast 
cancer can cause significant skin changes and breast pain, whereas 
radiotherapy to the pelvis can lead to vaginal fibrosis resulting in 
subsequent pain and decreased sensation.29 Androgen blockade is 
commonly used to treat prostate cancer but has been shown to 
contribute to both decreased libido and ED in males, with more 
than 80% of treated males reporting these symptoms 1 year after 
treatment.130 Radiation therapy for prostate cancer can also cause 
ED.76 Surgical prostatectomy has been associated with high rates 
of urinary incontinence and ED in males with additional possible 
long-term sequelae, including atrophy and fibrosis of the penile 
smooth muscle and degeneration of neural structures. 31,130,223 
Even after successful treatment of cancer, both males and females 
continue to report ongoing symptoms of sexual dysfunction, in -
cluding decreased desire and arousal, ED, dyspareunia, and anor-
gasmia.11,77,110 Patients’ sexual function may also be significantly 
impacted by changes in body image, fear of recurrence, and 
changed relationship dynamics.76
Chronic Pain
The reasons for sexual dysfunction in patients with chronic pain 
are multifactorial, often related to physiologic, pharmacologic, 
and psychological factors. 189 In one study, 73% of patients with 
chronic pain had pain-related difficulty with sexual activity. The 
reasons for this included decreased arousal, positioning issues, 
exacerbations of pain, low confidence, performance worries, and 
relationship issues. 8 In another study, sexual problems were re -
ported in 46% of patients with low back pain.79 Females with low 
back pain have been shown to have decreased frequency of sexual 
activity, more pain during sexual intercourse, and decreased sexual 
desire compared with males with low back pain or patients of ei -
ther gender with neck pain.157 Sexual dysfunction in patients with 
chronic pain has been correlated most strongly with the presence 
of depression, poor coping skills, and shorter pain duration. 148 
Many of the medications used to treat chronic pain (including 
opioids, antidepressants, and anticonvulsants) can independently 
contribute to sexual dysfunction.255
Rheumatologic Disease
Osteoarthritis (OA) impacts sexual function primarily through as-
sociated joint pain, stiffness, and fatigue with the hip joint being the 
most commonly implicated in leading to sexual difficulties.36,64,179 
T otal hip replacement often significantly improves sexual func -
tioning; in one study, 65% of patients found relief from sexual 
difficulties after surgery.249 It is important to instruct patients in 
proper positioning after joint replacement to reduce the risk of 
dislocation, and many surgeons instruct patients to refrain from 
sexual intercourse for 1 to 2 months after hip replacement for this 
reason.237 Hip replacement and arthroscopy carry a risk of injury 
to the pudendal nerve with subsequent urinary and/or sexual 
dysfunction; however, the risk is low, and the vast majority of 
these cases resolve on their own.106
Rheumatoid arthritis (RA), similar to OA, can cause problems 
in sexual functioning through joint pain, stiffness, and fatigue. 183 
RA in males has also been shown to be associated with decreased 
sexual desire and ED during periods of active inflammation. 102 
One study of females with RA identified 62% with difficulties in 
sexual performance caused by joint pain and stiffness and 92% 
with diminished sexual desire or satisfaction. Difficulties in sexual 
performance were related to overall level of disability and hip in -
volvement, whereas decreased sexual desire and satisfaction were 
correlated more with perceived pain, age, and depression. 2
Amputation
Patients with limb amputation usually have preserved physiologic 
sexual functioning unless a comorbid condition (diabetes or  
cardiac disease) or medication side effects play a significant detri -
mental role.117 However, their sexual life can be significantly af -
fected by a variety of factors associated with their amputation, 
including depression, poor self-esteem and body image, phantom 
sensations and pain, problems with balance and movement, and 
difficulties with body positioning during sexual activity. 228,270 A 
study of males with lower limb amputation reported ED in nearly 
70% of patients and orgasmic dysfunction in over 60%.38 Preser-
vation of the knee joint can be helpful for maintaining balance 
during sexual intercourse, although transfemoral amputees can 
use a pillow to aid in positioning. 48 Upper limb amputees would 
benefit from side-lying or supine positioning to allow for free 
movement of both the intact arm and the residual limb.237 Sexual 
dissatisfaction rates range from 13% to 75% among amputees 
with increased levels of sexual dysfunction despite preserved sex -
ual desire. Therefore this topic should be integrated into postam-
putation reintegration counseling.38,93,117
Diabetes Mellitus
Diabetes mellitus (DM) is one of the major comorbidities affect -
ing people with disabilities because it is a significant risk factor for 
stroke and amputation. DM plays a clear role in the development 
of sexual dysfunction in males. ED is three times more common 
in males with DM than in the general population, with preva -
lence estimates ranging from 35% to 75%. 27,158,245 Premature 
ejaculation and hypoactive sexual desire disorder have also been 
documented in 40% and 25%, respectively, of males with diabe -
tes.158 Diabetes-related sexual dysfunction in males is strongly 
correlated with glycemic control, duration of disease, and burden 
of diabetic complications. 27 The pathogenesis of sexual dysfunc -
tion in DM is likely to be multifactorial, including vasculopathy, 
autonomic neuropathy, and diminished nitric oxide production, 
which leads to decreased neurogenic vasodilation.12,245
Diabetes in females has a more ambiguous effect on sexual 
functioning because research is limited and at times contradic -
tory.179 The most commonly reported condition is sexual arousal

tom.12 Low sexual desire, decreased clitoral sensation, orgasmic 
dysfunction, and dyspareunia have also been infrequently re -

females with diabetes have been analyzed, and a far different pic -

been previously shown for cardiovascular comorbidity, depres -

Cardiac Disease
Cardiovascular disease is another common comorbidity in patients 
with disabilities. Hypertension, coronary artery disease, and con -
gestive heart failure have all been associated with increased preva-
lence of sexual dysfunction. 30,37,245 Hypertension is the most 
prevalent comorbidity among males with ED. 49,245 ED has been 
noted in 40% to 95% of males with hypertension and is correlated 
with duration of hypertensive disease.85,174 Control of blood pres-
sure often leads to restoration of erectile function. 179 ED is also 
common in cardiac disease with a prevalence of 42% to 75%. 198 
ED is often one of the earliest indicators of cardiovascular disease, 
and males (with or without disabilities) who present with ED with 
no obvious cause should be worked up for this potentially deadly 
comorbidity.245 It has been reported that 25% to 63% of females 
with cardiac disease experience sexual dysfunction, including de -
creased sexual desire, vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, decreased geni-
tal sensation, and decreased orgasmic ability.3,30,245
Psychological factors associated with cardiac disease also con -
tribute to sexual dysfunction. Cardiac disease, especially after 
myocardial infarction, is often associated with depression and 
anxiety.90,101,205 Other psychological factors that are likely to play 
a role include fear of recurrence of cardiac symptoms or fear of 
death with resumption of sexual activity.111 Cardiovascular deaths 
during sexual intercourse are actually very rare, with the rate in 
males estimated to be 0.2 per 100,000 and the risk in females  
12 times lower than in males.193
Many cardiovascular medications (including antihypertensives 
and digoxin) have been known to contribute to the sexual dys -
function seen in cardiac disease.255
Depression
Depression is one of the most common comorbidities in patients 
with all types of disabilities, and it has been shown to contribute 
to sexual dysfunction in a variety of ways, including medical, 
pharmacologic, and psychosocial. 56 There has been intense re -
search and publicity about the sexual side effects of antidepressant 
medications, but untreated depression has also been strongly cor-
related with sexual dysfunction.226 Clinically, it can be difficult to 
distinguish whether a patient’s sexual dysfunction is secondary to 
depression itself or to the antidepressant medication. 15 In one 
study of 134 patients with major depressive disorder who were 
not taking any medication to treat their illness, hypoactive sexual 
desire was seen in 40% to 50% of males and females, decreased 
arousal was observed in 40% of females, ED was seen in 50% of 
males, and ejaculatory or orgasmic dysfunction was seen in 15% 
to 20% of males and females. 134 Psychosocial factors in patients 
with depression that are likely to play a role in the development 
of sexual dysfunction include interpersonal relationship difficul -
ties and poor body image and self-esteem. 56
Sexual Dysfunction Related to Medication 
Use in Individuals With Disability
Sexual dysfunction is a common side effect of many medications 
used routinely in patients with disabilities. It is important for the 
physician to maintain open communication with patients, under-
stand these sexual side effects, and explain the risks to patients 
before prescribing these drugs. If sexual dysfunction occurs sec -
ondary to the use of a medication, it is often necessary to switch 
to a different class of medications to regain the function that has 
been lost or altered. Additionally, clinicians can start a medica -
tion, such as a phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE-5) inhibitor, to coun -
teract the sexual side effects associated with the initial medication. 
Table 23.2 presents an overview of the sexual dysfunctions associ-
ated with various classes of medications commonly encountered 
in a physiatry practice.25,172,255 Medications that most commonly 
cause sexual side effects are listed in boldface in Table 23.2. It 
should be noted that most research in this area has been focused 
on male sexual dysfunction; therefore less is known about the ef -
fects of these medications on female sexual dysfunction.
Evaluation of Sexual Dysfunction
Sexual History Taking
The idea of obtaining a sexual history can be daunting to some 
physicians. Remember that patients’ quality of life can be signifi-
cantly improved if their physicians are not afraid to discuss this 
topic with them. Because sexuality is a sensitive topic, it is best to 
maintain an attitude of openness and flexibility throughout the 
interview. There are multiple validated tools for assessing sexual 
dysfunction.144 The models ALLOW , PLISSIT , and BETTER are 
three different approaches for facilitating such a conversation 
(Box 23.2).87,107,115
When asking a patient about sexual dysfunction, it is impor -
tant to assess the nature of the problem and the time course of the 
complaint (including whether it started in relation to a certain 
disability, medical comorbidity, or medication administration). It 
is equally vital to ascertain whether the patient is experiencing 
dissatisfaction or disruption of quality of life because of the sexual 
dysfunction. The Brief Sexual Symptom Checklist is a self-report 
tool that can be a useful adjunct to the physician’s comprehensive 
sexual history (Fig. 23.4).107
A thorough inquiry into the patient’s sexual dysfunction also 
includes obtaining information about the medical, sexual, and 
psychosocial history of the patient. 107,128 The basic medical his -
tory includes both medical and surgical history, medication use 
(including over-the-counter and herbal medications), substance 
use (including smoking, alcohol abuse, and recreational drug use), 
and family medical history. 87 Important medical conditions to 
inquire about include cardiovascular disease, diabetes, hyperten -
sion, hyperlipidemia, cancer, benign prostatic hypertrophy, or the 
presence of lower urinary tract symptoms, neurologic diseases 
(e.g., SCI, MS, TBI, and stroke), thyroid conditions, and endo -
crine deficiencies (e.g., hypogonadism, androgen insufficiency, or 
estrogen deficiency).28,91,107,140,224 In females, it is also necessary to 
ask about reproductive history, history of gynecologic disease 
(e.g., fibroids or endometriosis), and current menstrual status. 140

ALLOW, PLISSIT, and BETTER Models for Facilitating Communication About Sexuality
A—Ask the patient about sexual function and activity.
L—Legitimize the patient’s problem and acknowledge that sexual dysfunction 
is a relevant clinical issue.
L—Limitations in the evaluation of the sexual dysfunction should be identified.
O—Open up the discussion: Potentially refer the patient to a subspecialist.
W—Work together to develop goals and a treatment plan.
P—Permission should be obtained from the patient to discuss sexuality.
LI—Limited information should be given (e.g., about normal sexual function -
ing and effects of disability).
SS— Specific suggestions should be given about the patient’s particular complaint.
IT—Intensive therapy may be required, including referral to a subspecialist or 
therapist.
B—Bring up the topic of sexuality.
E—Explain that sexuality is important and that you are open to discussion of it 
with your patient.
T—Tell your patient about resources that you will use to assist them.
T—Time the discussion to the patient’s preference.
E—Educate the patient about the side effects of treatment medications and 
the disability itself.
R—Record that you had a conversation about sexuality in the patient’s medi -
cal record.

The basic sexual history includes age of first sexual experience, 
types of sexual practices, sex of partners, history of sexually trans-
mitted diseases, inquiry into safe sex practices, and type of birth 
control used. 18 It should never be assumed that the patient is 
heterosexual, and sex-neutral terms (such as “partner” or “spouse” 
instead of “boyfriend” or “wife”) are preferable until the relation-
ship has been clarified by the patient.
16,128
The psychosocial history relevant to sexual dysfunction in -
cludes a history of depression, anxiety, disordered sleep, or other 
psychiatric illness.
28 Major life stressors and relationship dynamics 
should also be discussed. 107,140 Any history of abuse (physical, 
sexual, verbal, or emotional), sexual trauma, or domestic violence 
should also be sensitively explored.
128
Physical Examination
Although physical examination findings can be normal in patients 
with sexual dysfunction, it is important to do a thorough evaluation 
to identify pathology and to provide patient education about what 
is normal for the disability or disease state.
87 A comprehensive ex-

In males, the genital examination consists of examining the 
penis for lesions and urethral position.224 The penis should also be 
palpated in the stretched position to detect fibrous plaques consis-
tent with Peyronie disease (an acquired disorder of the tunica albu-
ginea characterized by the formation of fibrous tissue and often 
accompanied by penile pain and deformity on erection). 28 The 
testes should be checked for size, masses, and position, and the 
rectum should be evaluated for sphincter tone, pelvic floor muscle 
tenderness and strength, prostate size, masses, and lesions.224,271
In the genital examination of females, it is important to check 
the appearance of external genitalia, looking for inflammation or 
atrophy of the vulva, episiotomy or childbirth laceration scarring 
or strictures, and external dermatologic lesions. 128,140 A vaginal 
examination with one or two fingers inserted into the vagina is a 
good way to assess many of the common problems associated with 
female sexual dysfunction. 271 Vaginismus can be appreciated by 
resistance or inability to insert a finger into the vagina.128 T ender-
ness of the levator ani and obturator internus muscles can be as -
sessed, as well as pelvic floor muscle strength, coordination, and 
hypotonicity or hypertonicity.87,271 The presence of pelvic organ 
prolapse should be noted. 132 Bimanual examination can be per -
formed to assess for uterine and adnexal abnormalities, including 
tenderness and masses. 128,140 Rectal examination should also be 
performed to assess for anal sphincter muscle tone, pelvic floor 
muscle tenderness, and voluntary and involuntary pelvic floor 
muscle contraction and relaxation ability.271
In both genders, the physician should check for signs of infec-
tion or sexually transmitted diseases, including discharge, rashes, 
or ulcerations.128
Diagnostic Evaluation
Many laboratory tests and diagnostic procedures can be used to 
determine the pathologic origin of a patient’s sexual dysfunction. 
In general, the selection of treatment options is based on the pre-
senting complaint and not on the etiology behind the sexual 
dysfunction. Consequently, complicated diagnostic testing is usu-
ally unnecessary. Recommended laboratory testing for all males 
and females with sexual dysfunction includes complete blood cell 
count, chemistry panel, fasting glucose, and fasting lipid pro -
file.107,128,178 Other laboratory testing can be warranted on the 
basis of history and physical examination findings, including 
thyroid studies and serum-free testosterone, prolactin, and pros -
tate-specific antigen levels.7,28,107,180,224 Measurement of other sex 
hormones, such as estrogen, follicle-stimulating hormone, lutein-
izing hormone, or total testosterone, has been shown to have far 
less utility in a majority of cases. 7 In females, androgen levels do 
not correlate with sexual dysfunction and are not currently rec -
ommended for diagnosis. 69,142 Vaginal wet mount testing or 
screening for gonorrhea, chlamydia, or HIV can be done if infec-
tion is clinically suspected.128
For males with ED, a variety of specialized diagnostic proce -
dures are used to determine the specific etiology of disease, usually 
in preparation for surgical treatment.107 The use of these tests has 
declined since the advent of oral PDE-5 inhibitor medications 
caused by their ease of use, high level of effectiveness, and low  
side effect profile. The procedures include penile color duplex  
ultrasound, nocturnal penile tumescence monitoring, pharmaco -
arteriography, pharmacocavernosometry (PHCAS) or pharmaco -
cavernosography (PHCAG), and electrodiagnostic testing. 95 Pe-
nile color duplex ultrasound is the most practical and commonly 
used diagnostic modality. It is a good tool to diagnose vasculo -
genic ED and is minimally invasive. 95 Nocturnal penile tumes -
cence monitoring measures sleep-related erections and has tradi -
tionally been used to distinguish psychogenic and organic 
ED.95,107 Penile pharmacoarteriography, PHCAS or PHCAG, 
and electrodiagnostic testing, such as dorsal nerve stimulation or 
somatosensory evoked potentials, are more invasive and time 
consuming, so they are rarely used.95,242
In females, pelvic ultrasound is indicated if uterine or adnexal 
pathologic disorders are suspected. The use of objective measures 
for genital blood flow, such as vaginal photoplethysmography, and 
advanced imaging techniques, such as functional magnetic reso -
nance imaging, are currently limited to the research setting. 7,149
Treatment of Sexual Dysfunction
Male Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder
Limited research has been done on hypoactive sexual desire disorder 
in males, and thus little is known about treatment options. Second-
ary hypoactive sexual desire in males is thought to develop most 
frequently in response to another type of sexual dysfunction, usu-
ally ED or premature ejaculation.165 It follows that treatment of the 
primary sexual dysfunction would improve desire. Secondary hypo-
active sexual desire disorder is also often related to medication side 
effects, and thus changing the drug dose or category will often im-
prove the patient’s sexual drive. Primary hypoactive sexual desire is 
thought to typically relate to a “sexual secret,” such as a variant 
arousal pattern (e.g., a male who is aroused by internet pornogra-
phy but not by his partner), a preference for masturbatory sex over 
partner sex, a history of poorly processed sexual trauma, or conflict 
about sexual orientation.165 T reating these psychosocial factors and 
getting patients to be honest with themselves and their partner 
about their true motivations will be helpful.
Hypogonadism and low testosterone levels certainly contribute 
to hypoactive sexual desire in males, and treatment with testoster-
one supplementation is beneficial.171 It is less clear whether treat-
ment with testosterone in eugonadal males with hypoactive sexual 
desire disorder can improve their sexual drive, but at least two 
studies have shown this to be the case. 14,188
Erectile Dysfunction
The treatment of ED was revolutionized in 1998 with the US 
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval of sildenafil, the 
first of the now ubiquitous PDE-5 inhibitors, which have low side 
effect profiles and excellent efficacy across the spectrum of disease 
states that cause ED. 156,181 There are four PDE-5 inhibitors cur -
rently approved for use in the United States: sildenafil (Viagra), 
vardenafil (Levitra), tadalafil (Cialis), and avanafil (Stendra). Ava-
nafil received FDA approval in 2012 and may prove to have the 
earliest onset of action of available PDE-5 inhibitors. 196 Other 
new PDE-5 inhibitors, such as lodenafil, udenafil, and mirode -
nafil, have shown promise in meta-analyses of recent clinical trials 
and are currently available for use in a few countries. 75,78,108,196
Sildenafil and vardenafil have similar pharmacokinetics, with 
onset of action within 30 to 120 minutes and duration of efficacy 
of 4 to 5 hours. 80 Tadalafil has an onset of action of 30 to 60 
minutes and duration of efficacy of 12 to 36 hours. 80 High-fat 
intake is known to impede the action of sildenafil and vardenafil 
significantly.196 Tadalafil has been approved for on-demand or 
daily dosing and is effective for the concomitant treatment of 
symptoms related to ED and benign prostatic hyperplasia. 196

PDE-5 inhibitors have been studied and proven effective in pa-
tients with cardiovascular disease, hypertension, diabetes, SCI, 
MS, and depression.
80,196 These medications have been reported 
to improve erectile function significantly as evidenced by success-
ful vaginal penetration in as many as 79% to 87% of patients.
196 
Males with SCI achieve up to 80% success rates. 70,98 Side effects 
reported with PDE-5 inhibitors include headache, flushing, rhi -
nitis, back pain, and hearing loss.80 Nonarteritic anterior ischemic 
optic neuropathy has been reported, but to date, no causal link 
has been confirmed to PDE-5 inhibitor use.
196 These medications 
are strictly contraindicated in patients taking nitrates for chest 
pain.
156 In the setting of documented underlying hypogonadism, 
concurrent treatment with testosterone replacement therapy in -
creases the effectiveness of PDE-5 inhibitors.178,196
PDE-5 inhibitors are known to be less effective in patients 
with very severe ED, uncontrolled diabetes with neuropathy, se -
vere vascular disease, and in those who have undergone radical 
prostatectomy. Currently, few other oral agents show treatment 
benefit. Those available include sublingual apomorphine and yo -
himbine.
156 Bremelanotide, a melanocortin receptor agonist, was 
efficacious in multiple clinical trials, including those in patients 
who had been unsuccessfully treated with sildenafil.
215,227 Future 
oral therapies potentially include other melanocortin receptor 
agonists, dopamine receptor agonists, l-arginine, and Rho-kinase 
inhibitors.
108,196 Bupropion, given in a 6-week course, has been 
shown to be effective in treating both hypoactive sexual desire and 
ED in methadone-dependent male individuals with methadone 
emergent sexual dysfunction.
272
Second-line treatments after PDE-5 inhibitor treatment failure 
include intracavernosal injection therapy, intraurethral alprostadil 
(medicated urethral system for erection [MUSE]) therapy, topical 
alprostadil, low-intensity extracorporeal shock wave therapy  
(LI-ESWT), and vacuum constriction devices.
LI-ESWT is thought to address ED by inducing neovascular -
ization and increasing blood flow in cavernosal tissues.
258 It has 
been shown in several studies to be a safe and effective treatment 
modality for mild to moderate ED.
131,272 However, it has not yet 
been approved by the FDA, and more studies are needed.
Intracavernosal penile injections have been used for decades 
with treatment satisfaction rates of 87% to 93.5%, relatively low 
adverse effects, and rapid onset of action ( Fig. 23.5).
80,156 How-
ever, considerable training is required for effective use. High dis -
continuation rates have been noted secondary to penile pain, and 
complications that can occur include priapism and P eyronie dis-
ease.
80 The most commonly injected medications include various 
combinations of FDA-approved alprostadil and the off-label use 
of papaverine and phentolamine.
156
• Fig. 23.5 Penile injection.
Intraurethral treatment of alprostadil with the MUSE system 
can be an alternative in patients who cannot take PDE-5 inhibi -
tors and who do not want to try more intracavernosal injection 
therapy (Fig. 23.6). Its efficacy ranges from 37% to 53%, and 
penile pain is a common complaint.
196 It is rarely associated with 
hypotension and syncope. 80,156 T opical alprostadil formulations 
have undergone clinical trials with modest efficacy results, but this 
treatment is not currently recommended as monotherapy for 
ED.
196 Side effects include penile burning and partner vaginal 
pain.108 Studies have shown success with PDE-5 inhibitor and 
alprostadil (intracavernosal, intraurethral, or topical) combina-
tion therapy for patients who have previously failed either treat -
ment as a monotherapy.
176
Vacuum constriction devices apply a negative pressure to draw 
blood into the corpora cavernosa, which is then retained by  
the application of a constriction band at the base of the penis  
(Fig. 23.7).156 Efficacy rates are as high as 90%, but satisfaction 
rates tend to be lower because of the unnatural appearance of the 
erection, penile pain, and trapped ejaculate.
80,196 Constriction 
devices are contraindicated in patients with a history of severe 
bleeding disorder, priapism, or severe penile curvature.
196 Antico-
agulant therapy is a relative contraindication.156 These devices are 
often preferred in males who do not want to take medications. 
The constriction band cannot be left in place longer than 30 min-
utes because of the risk of ischemia, especially in patients who are 
insensate, such as those with SCI.
80,196
Third-line treatments for ED include surgical options, such as 
penile prostheses (Fig. 23.8). Penile prostheses come in two types: 
semirigid (malleable) and inflatable. Semirigid prostheses have 
malleable silicone elastomer rods with central metal cables that 
can be bent or straightened to produce erection.
80 Inflatable pros-
theses have cylinders that are implanted into the corpora caver -
nosa, a reservoir with fluid, and a pump that is placed in the 
scrotum.
80 Erection is achieved by compressing the scrotal pump 
to transfer fluid from the reservoir to the cylinders. 80 Infection 
rates have been reduced to approximately 1% because of antibi -
otic and hydrophilic coatings that are now available on the pros -
theses.80 Mechanical failure rates are 5% at 1 year, 20% by 5 years, 
and 50% at 10 years.156
Emerging treatments for ED continue to diversify, focusing on 
both central and peripheral mechanisms as well as innovative 
physical therapies. Central neurochemical peptides, such as mela-
notan II (a synthetic analogue of the peptide hormone alpha-  
melanocyte–stimulating hormone) and bremelanotide, demonstrate 
potential by triggering erections and enhancing sexual desire without 
the need for sexual stimulation, although they come with side effects 
such as nausea.

Premature Ejaculation
The mainstay of treatment until recently for premature ejacula -
tion was cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and psychological 
counseling. Various CBT approaches include the start-pause and 
frenulum squeeze techniques, as well as experimentation with 
positioning, rhythm, speed, breathing, and depth of penile pene -
tration.
6,190,210 These strategies have success rates of up to 70% in 
the short term, but long-term treatment satisfaction has only been 
reported at 25% to 60%.
210 Cognitive behavioral approaches to 
treatment are often very time consuming, expensive, and per -
ceived as intrusive and mechanistic, affecting intimacy and spon-
taneity during a sexual encounter.
190,210
Although no medications are FDA approved for treating prema-
ture ejaculation, many pharmacologic approaches have been well 
studied and show significant benefit. (Note that the following 
medications are used off-label for this condition.) Selective sero -
tonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are the most commonly used, 
with paroxetine being the most effective, followed by fluoxetine and 
sertraline.
6,212 The tricyclic antidepressant clomipramine has also 
been used successfully, but anticholinergic side effects limit its toler-
ance.6,190 Both SSRIs and clomipramine are usually given daily 
because of their slow onset of action (5 hours), long half-lives, and 
long treatment time (up to 4 weeks) to achieve a steady state.
6,190,212
On-demand treatment for premature ejaculation has been the 
holy grail of research into this disorder, and some recent advances 
have been promising. Conventional SSRIs have been studied for 
as-needed use, with less profound effects on IELT vs. daily dos -
ing.
6,212 Dapoxetine is a short-acting SSRI developed specifically for 
on-demand use for premature ejaculation. It achieves peak plasma 
concentration within 1 hour and has a half-life of 1.5 hours.
190 It 
has been shown to significantly increase IELT and improve pa -
tient satisfaction.133,190 Dapoxetine has been approved for use in 
several countries but is not yet available in the United States. 7 
T ramadol has also been shown to be an excellent on-demand 
treatment for premature ejaculation, with one study showing a 
13-fold increase in mean time to ejaculation.
6,212,214,269 Longer 
term safety studies and comparison to SSRIs are needed. T opical 
agents, such as lidocaine or EMLA cream, can be helpful by de -
creasing sensory perception in the penis, thereby prolonging 
ejaculation.
6,212 In the absence of concurrent ED, PDE-5 inhibi -
tors are contraindicated in premature ejaculation treatment.6
Delayed Ejaculation, Anejaculation,  
and Anorgasmia in Males
Assisted ejaculation methods that have proved effective for fertility 
treatment in males with SCI, MS, and other disabilities include 
penile vibratory stimulation (PVS) and rectal probe electroejacu -
lation (EEJ) (Figs. 23.9 and 23.10).
246 PVS is the most commonly 
used technique because it is able to produce superior sperm qual -
ity, is more comfortable and preferable to patients, and can be 
used in a home setting.
42,62,203 PVS produces ejaculation in only 
60% to 80% of cases, whereas EEJ is 80% to 100% successful.203 
EEJ is usually only used in patients in whom PVS has been unsuc-
cessful because it produces lower quality semen, must be con -
ducted in a physician’s office setting, and often requires the use of 
anesthesia to perform the procedure and retrieve ejaculate.
42,62,203 
novel PDE-5 inhibitors, such as SLx-2101, which offer different 
administration methods and improved pharmacokinetics.
137 Ad-
ditionally, therapies such as stem cell (SC) treatments are being 
explored, with clinical trials indicating improved penile function 
in ED patients through various forms of SC administration. More-
over, physical modalities such as low-intensity shock wave therapy 
and low-intensity pulsed ultrasound are gaining attention for their 
noninvasive nature and promising results in improving penile he-
modynamics and tissue remodeling.

Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder
When considering treatment for females with low sexual desire or 
arousal, it is important to remember that the sexual response cycle 
of females is very different from that of males. Many females ex -
perience only responsive, as opposed to spontaneous, sexual de -
sire.19 The first step in treating a female with a complaint of low 
sexual desire is educating the patient as to what is normal for fe -
males because there are many misconceptions fueled by popular 
culture about what females should feel.87 Referring the patient to 
the work of Basson may facilitate reassurance and improved un -
derstanding.17-19 After education, the next intervention level in -
cludes modification of potentially contributing factors such as 
lack of lubrication, menopausal vasomotor symptoms, pelvic 
pain, sleep dysfunction, or depression.58
Despite limited research, psychotherapy remains a mainstay 
for the treatment of female sexual interest/arousal disorder. In one 
of the few empirically tested outcome studies, mindfulness-based 
CBT improved sexual desire and arousal in gynecologic cancer 
survivors.47 Sexual behavioral techniques, sex therapy, and couples 
counseling (originally conceived by Masters and Johnson)160 have 
been shown to be beneficial by helping to reduce anxiety and ex -
aggerated sexual expectations.160,164
Flibanserin oral tablets and bremelanotide injectables are cur -
rently the only FDA-approved medications for treating general -
ized acquired hypoactive sexual desire disorder in premenopausal 
females. Flibanserin, categorized as a multifunctional serotonin 
agonist and antagonist, typically elicits a response in about 50% 
of treated females, with efficacy potentially taking up to 8 weeks 
to manifest. 73,132,256 Although clinical trials in postmenopausal 
females have shown comparable effectiveness, flibanserin is not 
currently approved by the FDA for this demographic. 232 Con -
versely, the bremelanotide injection operates by activating melano-
cortin receptors, albeit its precise mechanism remains unknown. 
Clinical trials demonstrated that patients using injectable bremel-
anotide experienced increased sexual desire scores and decreased 
distress scores compared with placebo, although common side 
effects such as nausea and vomiting were reported. However, cau-
tion is advised for individuals with high blood pressure or cardio-
vascular disease, and it should not be used concomitantly with 
certain medications, such as naltrexone.163
The other major pharmacologic treatment for female hypoac -
tive sexual desire is testosterone therapy. Many treatment prepara-
tions are available, but a transdermal patch is the most commonly 
studied and prescribed method. 192 There have been studies that 
have demonstrated the effectiveness of transdermal testosterone 
for increasing sexual desire and satisfaction in postmenopausal 
females or surgically menopausal females who have had bilateral 
oophorectomies.43,84,230 Less robust data exist in premenopausal 
females, and potential fetal exposure remains a concern. 67,142 A 
recent systematic review failed to show correlation between serum 
testosterone levels and libido in premenopausal females. 199 Side 
effects are usually related to masculinization, such as hirsutism 
and acne. The FDA has not approved the use of testosterone to 
treat female sexual interest/arousal disorder, and long-term safety 
data have not been established. 84 Theoretic concerns include ef -
fects on breast and endometrial cancer risks and cardiovascular 
health. Current available safety data, although not conclusive, are 
reassuring with regard to breast, endometrial, and cardiovascular 
outcomes.68,142 Further research is necessary.
Other medications that show promise for treating female hy -
poactive sexual desire include tibolone (a synthetic steroid), 
melanocortins, and combinations of testosterone with PDE-5 
inhibitors or buspirone. 74,142,164,227
Pharmacologic interventions for female arousal and lubrica -
tion disorders have been increasingly studied in recent years, 
yielding mixed results. Because the physiology of female arousal is 
thought to mirror male erectile function, there was significant 
hope that sildenafil would be an effective treatment. However, re-
search trials have been largely disappointing, especially compared 
with the success of PDE-5 inhibitors in males with ED. Although 
sildenafil has been well tolerated, the overall body of research  
to date indicates it does not benefit most females with generalized 
arousal disorder without a known organic cause. 24,164,229 For  
females with disabilities, PDE-5 inhibitors remain a viable treat -
ment option. T rials have shown positive results in small popula -
tions of females with acquired genital arousal disorders of  
an organic nature, such as premenopausal type 1 diabetes, SCI, 
MS, and those taking SSRIs. 53,164,187,241 More research is needed 
on PDE-5 inhibitors in females, both with and without acquired 
disabilities.
Other medications have shown more promise than sildenafil 
for treating female arousal disorders, especially in postmenopausal 
females. These include melanocortins, phentolamine, l-arginine, 
and alprostadil. Acquired arousal disorder in postmenopausal fe -
males is often treated with systemic or local estrogen therapy to 
improve vaginal lubrication and blood flow. 141 Mechanical de -
vices can also enhance arousal, such as vibratory stimulators and 
the Eros Clitoral Therapy Device—a clitoral vacuum approved by 
the FDA to boost female arousal and orgasm. 74,119,135,211,213,227
Female Orgasmic Dysfunction
Female orgasmic dysfunction, when not accompanied by an 
arousal disorder, is typically considered a psychogenic issue. Effec-
tive treatments include CBT , which aims to reduce anxiety and 
shift sexual attitudes and thoughts; sensate focus therapy, which 
involves guided exercises to enhance sexual intimacy with a part -
ner; and directed masturbation, which educates females on the 
normalcy and health benefits of self-stimulation to discover what 
works for them. 87,141 These behavioral treatments have been 
shown to be effective in 60% of females. 170 Bupropion has been 
shown in at least one study to help orgasmic dysfunction in fe -
males.175 As with decreased arousal, mechanical devices can help 
with the attainment of orgasm.33
Genitopelvic Pain/Penetration Disorder
Dyspareunia and vaginismus have various organic and psycho -
genic causes, and proper treatment depends on identifying the 
underlying etiology. Diagnosing and treating any underlying 
pelvic pathology is crucial. The management of pelvic floor dys -
function and vulvodynia, major contributors to genitopelvic 
pain/penetration disorder, is detailed in Chapter 38.
Summary
Disability does not lessen the significance of sexuality in an indi -
vidual's overall quality of life. By thoroughly understanding the
